s__Faecalibacterium_prausnitzii.what_it_does_plain: Makes butyrate — the main fuel for the cells lining your colon, and a powerful anti-inflammatory compound. Think of it as the gut's internal fire extinguisher.
s__Faecalibacterium_prausnitzii.sex_specific_note: No significant sex difference in baseline F. prausnitzii; however, the mucosal barrier protection provided by butyrate becomes especially important during perimenopause, when estrogen decline already reduces mucosal protection through a separate pathway.

s__Akkermansia_muciniphila.primary_function: Mucus layer maintenance, GLP-1 stimulation, gut barrier integrity
s__Akkermansia_muciniphila.what_it_does_plain: Lives in your gut's protective mucus layer and keeps it thick and healthy. Also triggers GLP-1 — a hormone that helps regulate blood sugar and energy. Think of it as the gut wall's maintenance crew.
s__Akkermansia_muciniphila.sex_specific_note: Akkermansia levels are directly modulated by estrogen. Estrogen maintains the mucus layer that Akkermansia colonises. As estrogen declines in perimenopause (typically age 38–55), Akkermansia levels fall. This creates a compounding effect: lower estrogen → thinner mucus → less Akkermansia → even thinner mucus.

s__Escherichia_coli.primary_function: Normally a minor commensal; opportunistic bloomer when gut ecology is disrupted
s__Escherichia_coli.what_it_does_plain: Most E. coli strains are completely harmless — they've lived in human guts for millions of years. But at 8% they're running 20x above the healthy median, which is a signal that the gut ecosystem is out of balance. E. coli also produces an enzyme that recycles estrogen.
s__Escherichia_coli.sex_specific_note: E. coli is a major producer of beta-glucuronidase — the enzyme that deconjugates (reactivates) estrogens excreted in bile. At 8%, Susan's E. coli is producing significantly elevated levels of this enzyme, likely increasing estrogen reabsorption. During perimenopause, when estrogen is already fluctuating, this recycling loop can amplify hormonal variability and contribute to irregular energy, bloating, and mood shifts.

s__Bacteroides_vulgatus.what_it_does_plain: One of the most common bacteria in the Western gut. Excellent at breaking down complex plant carbohydrates and animal glycoproteins. At 22% it's dominant, which is typical for an omnivorous diet — not a concern in itself.

s__Prevotella_copri.what_it_does_plain: Specialises in breaking down plant-based carbohydrates — particularly the fibres found in wheat and oats. High in people eating lots of grains and vegetables. The story here is nuanced.
high_associated_with: Insulin resistance when gut ecology is imbalanced

s__Bifidobacterium_longum.primary_function: Immune regulation, B-vitamin synthesis, lactate production
s__Bifidobacterium_longum.what_it_does_plain: An immune system educator. Helps train your gut's immune cells to distinguish friend from foe. Also produces vitamins. At 2.1% it's slightly below the healthy median — not alarming but worth supporting.
s__Bifidobacterium_longum.sex_specific_note: Bifidobacterium levels naturally decline with age. At 42, some decline from childhood levels is expected, but the 2.1% suggests borderline low for an adult.

s__Roseburia_intestinalis.what_it_does_plain: Another important butyrate producer. Works alongside Faecalibacterium to keep the colon lining healthy. At 1.8%, it's slightly low — adds to the overall picture of reduced butyrate production.

s__Eubacterium_rectale.primary_function: Butyrate production, cross-feeding with other gut bacteria
s__Eubacterium_rectale.what_it_does_plain: One of the major butyrate producers in the human gut. At 2.9% it's below the healthy median of 4.8% — another contributor to the overall butyrate deficit picture.

s__Methanobrevibacter_smithii.primary_function: Hydrogen removal via methanogenesis, improves fermentation efficiency
s__Methanobrevibacter_smithii.what_it_does_plain: An archaeon (not a bacterium — an entirely different domain of life) that removes hydrogen gas from the gut. This actually improves the efficiency of bacterial fermentation by preventing hydrogen buildup. At 1.3% it's at a healthy level.
high_associated_with: Some association with constipation at very high levels
